query: what is the DOM interview question

competitors.label: Document Object Model (DOM)
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical MDN page for what the DOM is, how the in-memory tree works, and how scripts interact with nodes and objects.
theirStrengths: MDN is authoritative on the DOM as an in-memory tree of nodes and objects and on how DOM APIs connect documents to scripts.
theirGaps: The page is broader and less explicit about selector timing bugs, stale UI, and reflow or repaint costs that show up in debugging conversations.

realWorldUseCases: If you query too early, mutate the DOM too often, or misunderstand which node actually changed, you get timing bugs, stale UI, and expensive reflow or repaint work in production.
followUpConfusion: The DOM differs from the BOM, which controls the browser’s environment, not the document itself.

realWorldUseCases: MDN defines the DOM well, but it stays closer to the general programming interface explanation than to one concrete production debugging story.
actionableExamples: It includes direct API examples like querySelectorAll and shows how the tree is represented, so the example criterion stays competitive.
followUpConfusion: The page is lighter on the next questions interviewers often ask, such as DOM versus BOM, querying too early, and why repeated mutations turn into render work.

actionableExamples: Keep one shorter example that contrasts a safe batched update with repeated DOM writes that trigger extra layout work.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical definition, but our page is more interview- and debugging-friendly because it translates the DOM tree into timing, stale-UI, and reflow pitfalls instead of stopping at the API definition.

competitors.label: HTML Interview Questions and Answers
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd's HTML interview bank is directly relevant and is the strongest interview-prep comparator for general HTML fundamentals such as DOM behavior, semantics, and accessibility.
theirStrengths: It is a strong interview-prep source with detailed HTML answers and worked examples across multiple HTML fundamentals.
theirGaps: The bank page is not dedicated to the DOM mental model and is therefore less focused on one exact debugging-oriented answer.

realWorldUseCases: That distinction matters when you debug real UI issues.
followUpConfusion: If you query too early, mutate the DOM too often, or misunderstand which node actually changed, you get timing bugs, stale UI, and expensive reflow or repaint work in production.

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd covers HTML interview topics well, but the category page is less anchored to one concrete DOM debugging scenario.
actionableExamples: It includes detailed answers and code for many HTML topics, so the example bar remains close.
followUpConfusion: The landing page is less explicit about DOM timing, DOM versus BOM, and render-cost follow-ups than our dedicated answer.

actionableExamples: Keep the DOM-tree example and one DOM mutation example close together so the answer stays skimmable under interview time pressure.

competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd is strong as a broad HTML interview hub, but our page is more specific about the under-the-hood DOM model and the next debugging questions that usually follow.

competitors.label: DOM tree
competitors.selectionReason: javascript.info has a direct DOM tree article and is the strongest third-source comparator for the browser tree and node-relationship mental model.
theirStrengths: It is very strong on the DOM tree, parent-child relationships, and direct node traversal examples.
theirGaps: It is less explicit about production timing bugs, stale UI, and reflow or repaint consequences of repeated DOM changes.

realWorldUseCases: If you query too early, mutate the DOM too often, or misunderstand which node actually changed, you get timing bugs, stale UI, and expensive reflow or repaint work in production.
followUpConfusion: The DOM differs from the BOM, which controls the browser’s environment, not the document itself.

realWorldUseCases: javascript.info is excellent on tree relationships and browser parsing corrections, but it is lighter on concrete production debugging symptoms.
actionableExamples: Its DOM tree examples and traversal explanations are strong, so the example criterion stays close.
followUpConfusion: The article is less explicit about DOM versus BOM and about why frequent DOM writes often surface as reflow or repaint issues.

actionableExamples: Keep one shorter query-too-early snippet so the page keeps at least one API-level debugging example, not only conceptual framing.

competitors.winReason: javascript.info is very good on the DOM tree itself, but our page is more useful for interview follow-ups because it carries the discussion into timing bugs, stale UI, and DOM-versus-BOM confusion.
